task: We’ve got a stormy stretch coming up, and I’m trying to decide what’s worth doing before it turns into a headache. Can you help me make a practical prep list for the house, food, and backup power so I’m not scrambling later?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a practical pre-storm prep list, organized so you can work through it fast.

## House
- Bring in or secure loose outdoor items
- Charge phones, power banks, flashlights, battery packs
- Test flashlights and replace weak batteries
- Set out candles and matches/lighters if you use them
- Check sump pump is working
- Clear drains, gutters, and downspouts if needed
- Close and latch windows
- Move valuables up off the floor in low-risk leak areas
- Check for anything that could leak or drip if power goes out
- Make sure you know where the main water shutoff is

## Food and water
- Fill clean containers with water
- Check fridge/freezer thermometer
- Use or move older food to the front
- Freeze water bottles or containers to help keep freezer cold
- Stock easy no-cook food
- Make sure you have a manual can opener
- Check prescription refills and basic meds
- If you have formula, pet food, or special diet items, top those off too

## Backup power
- Test generator if you have one
- Check fuel age and condition
- Decide whether you actually want gasoline on hand
- Store fuel safely and only if it makes sense for your setup
- Check extension cords and make sure the gauge is appropriate for the load
- Put cords where you can reach them quickly
- Confirm you have a way to power the fridge/freezer, sump pump, or phone charging as needed
- Keep power banks fully charged

## Quick decision items
- Anything already damaged? Fix or move it now
- Anything that depends on power and would cause damage if it failed? Prioritize that
- Anything you’ll need in the first 12 hours of an outage? Put it in one easy-to-grab spot

If you want, I can turn this into a tighter one-page checklist you can print and keep with your flashlight and papers.

user_simulator.text: Pretty good, but it’s a little generic. I’d want it tighter and more specific to a house like mine — what to do first, what can wait, and what’s just busywork. I don’t need candles at all. Also, I’d rather have a generator/fuel note that doesn’t assume I’m storing a lot of gas like some fool. Give me the short version I can actually use before the weather gets ugly.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s the short, practical version.

## Do first
- Charge phones and power banks
- Test flashlights
- Check sump pump
- Set fridge/freezer thermometer
- Move important items up off the floor in any leak-prone area
- Bring in or secure loose outdoor items
- Close and latch windows
- Make sure you know where the main water shutoff is

## Do if you have time
- Fill a few clean water containers
- Put older food where you’ll use it first
- Freeze a few water bottles for the freezer
- Check prescription refills and basic meds
- Set out manual can opener
- Confirm you have easy no-cook food

## Backup power
- Test generator
- Check fuel age before you count on it
- Keep only a small, sensible amount of gasoline on hand if you actually rotate it
- If you won’t rotate it, don’t keep gas around just to have it
- Check extension cords and use the right gauge for the load
- Put the cord you’d need for the fridge, sump pump, or charging where you can grab it fast

## Skip unless needed
- Candles
- Extra rearranging
- Anything that doesn’t prevent damage, keep food cold, or keep the lights/phone/pump going

If you want, I can turn this into a plain one-page checklist with checkboxes.

user_simulator.text:
That’ll do. Short, plain, and not full of nonsense. The way you split it — do first, if you have time, skip — is about right. I don’t need the candles or the extra fuss.

The generator note is better too. I’m not keeping a big stash of gas around just to feel prepared. Small amount, rotated, or don’t bother. That’s the sensible part.

One-page checklist with boxes would be fine if you’re making it.
